submissions.biggestGap: The biggest gap is domain knowledge and bridging the business logic with technical execution. Because I am new to the team, my reliance on templates and external help stems largely from not yet fully understanding the underlying business rules, edge cases, and user expectations. I cannot effectively orchestrate agents, if I don't deeply understand the workflow i'm supposed to automate
submissions.plannedActions: Deep dive into one core business workflow. Instead of trying to learn everything at once, I will sit down with a subject matter expert (or review existing documentation/code) to map out one specific, critical business process end-to-end. My goal is to successfully translate that human-level business logic into clear system constraints and prompts for an agent, proving I understand why the rules exist.

submissions.biggestGap:
I rated long-running agents as 3 because, if we consider a long-running process as something that takes 4+ hours, I haven’t really had a use case where I could apply this type of skill effectively.

That said, the pattern of unattended tasks with explicit success/failure criteria and self-verification is something I consistently use in short- and mid-running processes.
submissions.plannedActions: Over the next month, I want to run one small experiment applying this pattern to a real task: define the agent roles, handoffs, shared artifacts, and success/failure checks. I don’t need a perfect orchestration system yet, but I want to start practicing the pattern more intentionally.
submissions.hackathonTakeaway: I wasn’t able to fully join the last Hackathon, but I did participate in part of the first session. My main takeaway was that when you provide enough engineering context about the system or domain, it makes the implementation flow much more naturally when using Harness
submissions.hackathonPlan: Recap where we left off in the last Hackathon and bring insights on how new system migrations should be led, so the whole team can work on the implementation with less friction

But we must continue to create more value with this and keep improving, so that AI works better and better and we achieve better results

submissions.biggestGap: Opportunities to have long running tasks, outside of the hackathon, regarding more complex designs and initiatives.
submissions.plannedActions: Ideally tackle a bunch of initiatives and harden the harness around these systems, for both the enduring developmeng of them and future merchant initiatives (i.e., pathing the way).
submissions.hackathonTakeaway: There needs to be more supervision in the exploratory phase. With the auth layer, roughly 90% of components were already available to consume, but the contract forced agents to reinvent them anyway. Even with a tight overall scope, implementation still took a long time, so validation depth also needs to be more tightly scoped per component going forward. On model choices, I plan to change our approach for worker agents, since many "smart workers" were stalling the process significantly; I want to test a new configuration, particularly around GLM-5.2 and MiniMax-M3 as worker models. Opus is too slow for orchestration in Droid, but performs well on smaller, scoped tasks in Claude Code (CC).
submissions.hackathonPlan: For this Hackathon, my focus is on: the model configuration changes for worker agents. I want to test GLM-5.2 and MiniMax-M3 as worker models. It's the most concrete, testable piece of work for a two day window. I'll also try applying tighter validation scoping, as I build, to see if it actually cuts implementation time. And finally, the supervision gap in the exploratory phase, is more of a process fix than something to build, so I'll treat it as a learning to carry forward rather than something to implement this round.

submissions.hackathonTakeaway:
Harness is as important to agent development as code coverage is to tests. This becomes even more important when multiple teammates are touching the same codebase, as even if agents generate good code, when they need to make a choice (e.g. filename convention) we can end up with conflicts or duplicated files.

Reviews from multiple teammates is very important to decrease risk of unexpected code reaching migration branch.
submissions.hackathonPlan: Improve communication with teammates and review anything to also enrich my context.
